title: Treble and the magic ring
subtitle: The two foundations every in-the-round crochet pattern depends on
excerpt: The treble is the workhorse stitch of British crochet. The magic ring is the cleanest way to start a piece in the round. Learn both together — every in-the-round pattern on the site assumes you know them.
sourceNotes: Therese de Dillmont, Encyclopedia of Needlework (1886) — crochet chapter, the long treble worked into a foundation chain (Project Gutenberg #20776). Weldon's Practical Crochet, Volume 1 (1885) — granny-square ring start that became the modern magic ring. Pauline Turner, How to Crochet (HarperCollins, 2008) — modern adjustable-loop technique cross-referenced for the contemporary phrasing.

recipeTools.notes: A 4 mm hook is the default British DK size. Anything from 3.5 mm to 5 mm works for a learning swatch.
For sewing in the starting tail when you finish the swatch.

glossaryTerms.definition: An adjustable starting loop that pulls closed once the first round is anchored. The cleanest way to start a piece in the round — no centre hole.
The short chain at the start of each new row or round that brings the working yarn up to the height of the next stitch. A treble uses a 3-chain turning chain that counts as the first treble.
Wrapping the working yarn around the hook from back to front before inserting the hook into the stitch. The move that gives a treble its height.

content.text: Two moves underpin most British crochet patterns: the treble stitch, which gives a fabric its height and structure, and the magic ring, which lets you start a piece in the round without a hole in the middle. Patterns on this site assume both. Learn them together on a 4 mm hook and a small ball of DK-weight cotton, and the rest of the library opens up.

A note on terminology before you start. UK "treble" is the same move that US patterns call "double crochet". The first time you read a pattern from a US source, the abbreviation "dc" will mean a different stitch from the one this site means by "dc". UK convention is the default here; the reader-side terminology toggle in the page header swaps the abbreviations if you prefer the US convention.

attrs.title: What you need for a learning swatch
attrs.body: Around 20 g of plain DK-weight cotton in a light colour (light shows the stitches; dark hides them). A 4 mm hook. A tapestry needle and a pair of small scissors. A flat surface and good light. That's it — a learning swatch shouldn't need a stash.

content.text: Part one: working a treble

Start with a slip knot on the hook. Make a starting chain of 12 — twelve chains plus the slip knot. The 12 stitches give you a 10-stitch row plus the 3-chain turning chain that counts as your first treble, and one extra to land the first treble in the right place.
Each treble follows the same four-beat sequence:
Yarn over (wrap the working yarn around the hook from back to front).
Insert the hook from front to back through the next chain. You now have a chain on the hook, the yarn-over loop, and the working yarn.
Yarn over again and pull a loop back through the chain. Three loops on the hook.
Yarn over and pull through the first two loops on the hook. Two loops left. Yarn over again and pull through the remaining two. One loop left on the hook — the treble is finished.
The first treble of any row uses the 3-chain
turning chain

in place of a worked treble. So on your starting chain, the first treble you work goes into the fifth chain from the hook — the three turning chains are chains 1, 2, and 3; chain 4 is the space; chain 5 is where the first worked treble lands.

Work one treble in each of the next 9 chains. You now have 10 trebles in the row — the 9 worked trebles plus the turning chain that counts as the first. Turn the work over. Chain 3 (counts as the first treble of row 2). Skip the first stitch (the previous turning chain) and work 1 treble in each of the next 9 stitches, ending with one treble in the top chain of the previous turning chain. 10 sts. Repeat for as many rows as you want to practise.
What a good treble looks like

A neat treble shows a clear V-shape at the top (the two loops of the stitch head), one or two visible bars in the middle of the post, and a straight base where it sits on the row below. About 12 mm tall in DK cotton at 4 mm hook tension. If your trebles look squashed, your tension is too tight — work the next row with the hook held a little looser. If they look loopy and uneven, your tension is too loose — try a 3.5 mm hook.
Part two: the magic ring

In-the-round patterns — granny squares, hexagons, mandalas, amigurumi — start at the centre and work outward. The old way was a starting chain joined into a ring with a slip stitch; that left a small visible hole in the middle. The
magic ring
replaces both moves. You start with an adjustable loop, work the first round into the loop, then pull the tail to close the loop. No hole.
The move has three stages. Form the loop. Work into the loop. Close the loop.
Form the loop

Hold the working yarn between your thumb and middle finger of your non-hook hand, with the tail pointing right (assuming a right-handed hook). Drape the working yarn over your index finger, then back across the tail, crossing the working yarn over the tail. You now have a loop with the working yarn crossing over the tail. The crossing point is where the hook will enter.

Insert the hook into the loop from front to back, catch the working yarn (yarn over), and pull a loop back through. You now have one loop on the hook. Chain 1 to lock the loop — this chain is not counted as the first stitch of the round. The loop you're holding is the magic ring; the working yarn coming off the hook will be worked into it.
Work into the loop

Whatever the pattern asks for — typically 12 trebles for a granny-square first round, 6 trebles for an amigurumi first round — is worked into the loop itself, around the two strands at the front of the ring. Insert the hook into the centre of the ring (not through any of the strands), yarn over, pull a loop back through, and continue the stitch as normal. Each new stitch threads under the same two strands.
When you've worked the round's stitches, you'll have a fan of stitches sitting on a loose loop. Don't close yet — you need the loop slack while you work.
Close the loop

Pick up the starting tail and pull. The loop tightens. The stitches you just worked move closer together. Pull until the centre closes completely — no hole — then anchor the round with a slip stitch into the top of the first stitch of the round (the top of the 3-chain turning chain, if the round started with a treble). The piece is now in the round; the magic ring is closed; the tail can be sewn in with a tapestry needle when you finish the piece.

attrs.title: Belt-and-braces for the magic ring
attrs.body: Some crocheters find the closed loop comes undone when they work the next round into it. The fix: after pulling the tail tight, sew the tail through one or two of the round-1 stitches with the tapestry needle before working round 2. The stitches lock the tail in place and the ring can't open. Sew the remaining tail in along the back of the piece when you finish.

content.text: Common mistakes

attrs.heading: When your swatch doesn't look right
attrs.intro: Five things that go wrong in the first treble swatch + magic ring, and the fix for each.

items.symptom: The treble swatch grows narrower row by row
items.cause: You worked the last treble of each row into the top of the previous turning chain — but on row 2 you skipped that chain. Each row lost one stitch.
items.fix: On every row after row 1, work the last treble of the row into the top of the previous turning chain. Count to 10 at the end of each row.

items.symptom: The treble swatch grows wider row by row
items.cause: You worked into both the previous turning chain and the last treble — adding a stitch each row.
items.fix: On every row after row 1, the first treble of the row sits on top of the second stitch of the previous row (skipping the first stitch — the turning chain that counted). 10 sts each row, every row.

items.symptom: The trebles look squashed and the swatch is stiff
items.cause: Tension too tight. Common when learning — the hand grips the hook too hard.
items.fix: Loosen the grip on both the hook and the working yarn. Try the same swatch on a 4.5 mm hook; if that fixes it, your default tension is firmer than DK-standard and you should use one hook size up from any pattern recommendation.

items.cause: You pulled the wrong tail — the working yarn loop instead of the starting tail.
items.fix: The closing tail is the short tail you left when you formed the slip knot. It comes out of the ring at the crossing point. Pull only that tail; the working yarn (the long one coming off the hook) stays where it is.

items.symptom: The closed magic ring keeps coming undone
items.cause: Slippery yarn (cotton, mercerised cotton) doesn't grip itself the way wool does. The closed loop slips open under tension.
items.fix: Sew the tail through one or two round-1 stitches with a tapestry needle before working round 2 (see the tip panel above). On very slippery yarn, work the first stitch of round 1 around both the magic ring and the tail at the same time — the tail gets locked into the first stitch as you work it.

content.text: What to try next

Once trebles and the magic ring feel natural, the whole granny-square family opens up — the granny square itself (the next anchor pattern), the granny hexagon, and the long-form Victorian doily patterns from Weldon's Practical Crochet. The treble is also the building block of every cluster, V-stitch, and shell in the British crochet stitch library; once the basic treble is reliable, those textured stitches are recombinations of the same four-beat sequence.
